Document [1] (Title: Cancer Screening -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes) Certain organizations, including the USPSTF and ACS, periodically review the available evidence and update their recommendations as more trials demonstrate the real survival and mortality benefits of cancer screening and early diagnosis. Primary healthcare providers should stay updated with changing screening guidelines. Primary care physicians are critical in determining screening eligibility, engaging in informed decision-making discussions, and coordinating care with specialists. Additionally, issues regarding insurance and affordability should be addressed at the institutional level and by nonprofit organizations, while patients should be guided to appropriate resources when available. Handouts distributed in clinics, pharmacies, and common public places, such as grocery stores, have significantly increased public awareness. Healthcare providers must now ensure they make appropriate recommendations for screening patients based on published guidelines, patient age, preferences, comorbidities, life expectancy, and risk factors.

Document [31] (Title: Prostate Cancer -- Pearls and Other Issues -- The Current United States Preventive Services Task Force Recommendation) Many professional organizations now have guidelines and recommendations regarding PSA screening for prostate cancer. Nonetheless, patients and family members should make this decision after thoroughly discussing the pros and cons of continuing screening.

Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70) BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.
